title: Bragg-enhanced time-domain Brillouin scattering from a propagating acoustic grating
authors: O. S. Ken, D. O. Horiachyi, V. L. Korenev, A. V. Trifonov, I. A. Akimov, D. R. Yakovlev, M. Bayer
abstract: Generation and detection of coherent phonons in semiconductors by femtosecond optical pulses is a powerful tool for high-frequency acoustic control of their electronic properties. Here, we demonstrate a propagating one-dimensional acoustic grating in bulk semiconductors using above-band-gap excitation by a train of laser pulses with high repetition rate of 1 GHz. This approach enables shaping of the coherent acoustic phonon spectrum and leads to a significant enhancement of Brillouin light scattering at selected probe wavelengths in pump-probe configuration. We demonstrate this effect at a cryogenic temperature of 5 K in prototypical semiconductor systems, namely bulk crystalline GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te, which serve as benchmark materials for the proposed method. The spectral dependence of the time-domain Brillouin light scattering amplitude exhibits resonant peaks at discrete probe wavelengths arising from Bragg reflection of the probe light by the propagating acoustic grating. A 10-30-fold resonant enhancement of the signal amplitude is observed for GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te, determined by the finite spectral width of the probe pulse. With further spectral narrowing, enhancements of the order ~ 100-150 are expected, set by the number N of strain pulses in the acoustic grating within the sample and ultimately limited by the material parameters and sample thickness.
summary: This work demonstrates a novel method to enhance the detection of coherent acoustic phonons in semiconductors by generating a propagating acoustic grating using a high-repetition-rate laser pulse train. By exploiting Bragg reflection, the Brillouin light scattering signal is resonantly enhanced by factors up to 30 times compared to single-pulse excitation. This technique provides a highly sensitive tool for studying high-frequency acoustic dynamics in solid-state materials.
main_problem: The study aims to enhance the detection of Brillouin light scattering (BLS) from coherent acoustic phonons in semiconductors by utilizing a propagating acoustic grating.
main_result: The multi-pulse approach generates a propagating acoustic grating, leading to a significant resonant enhancement of the BLS signal, observed to be 10-30-fold in GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te.
method: The technique involves using a high-repetition-rate laser pulse train to create the grating, followed by time-domain Brillouin scattering measurements in a pump-probe configuration.
model_or_system: The experiment is performed on bulk semiconductors (GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te) at cryogenic temperatures, utilizing a two-color differential reflectivity pump-probe setup.
key_observables: Resonant peaks in the spectral dependence of the TDBS amplitude, the acoustic phonon frequency (f_ac), and the enhancement factor.
important_parameters: The enhancement factor is limited by the number of strain pulses (N) and the finite spectral width of the probe pulse; the resonance condition is governed by the Bragg reflection relation.
main_assumptions: The analysis models the signal using a geometric series sum for the total scattered field, assuming normal incidence of the probe beam.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between conventional single-pulse and multi-pulse TDBS approaches, and show the spectral dependence of the enhanced signal.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from introducing the concept of coherent phonon generation to detailing the multi-pulse grating formation, deriving the enhanced signal mathematically via geometric series summation, and finally presenting experimental results on GaAs and (Cd,Zn)Te.
why_interesting: The use of periodic excitation to engineer a macroscopic optical/acoustic resonance (Bragg enhancement) is a powerful analogy to engineered quantum systems, particularly in cavity QED or photonic structures, demonstrating controlled enhancement of a fundamental physical signal.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the conventional and multi-pulse TDBS approaches. (a) In conventional TDBS, a single pump pulse generates a propagating strain pulse that scatters the probe light; interference between the scattered field and the probe light reflected from the sample surface gives rise to the TDBS signal. (b) In the multi-pulse approach, a high-repetition-rate (∼1 GHz) train of pump pulses generates a propagating acoustic grating. When the Bragg condition is satisfied, constructive interference of probe light scattered from successive grating planes leads to a pronounced enhancement of the TDBS signal amplitude.

Figure 2: Results for GaAs. (a) Oscillatory component of the transient signals measured at different probe photon energies hν (the exponential background has been subtracted, and the curves are vertically offset for clarity). The inset shows the raw signal at hν = EX = 1.515 eV. (b) Spectral dependence of the oscillatory signal amplitude (blue dots) revealing peaks which correspond to the interference orders M = 40 −43 and the exciton (X) line. Horizontal error bars indicate the probe spectral width. The solid blue curve shows a global fit given by the sum of Gaussian functions for the interference peaks and a Lorentzian function for the exciton. The dashed light-blue and red curves...

Figure 3: Results for (Cd,Zn)Te. (a) Variation of the oscillatory components of the transient signals along the contour of the M = 23 Bragg maximum (the exponential background has been subtracted, and the curves are vertically offset for clarity). Inset shows the FT spectrum of the oscillatory component of the signal measured at a probe photon energy hν = 1.5448 eV. (b) Spectral dependence of the oscillatory signal amplitude (blue dots) showing peaks which correspond to interference orders M = 21 −26 and their fit by Gaussian functions (light-blue solid line). Inset shows enlarged spectral region indicated by the bracket in the main panel: the oscillatory signal amplitude (blue dots...
Figure 4: Schematic illustration of Bragg reflection from a propagating acoustic grating, giving rise to multi- beam interference of the probe light scattered by the different acoustic pulses.
